title: Chaos Engineering & GameDays
description: Chaos engineering is the discipline of experimenting on a distributed system to build confidence in its ability to withstand turbulent conditions. Originated at Netflix (Chaos Monkey), adopted by Amazon (GameDays), Google (DiRT), and Microsoft. This file covers the scientific method of chaos engineering, steady-state hypothesis, blast radius, GameDay design patterns, and how SAP's downtime patterns map to chaos experiments.

sections.title: The Scientific Method of Chaos Engineering

description.value:
Chaos engineering is NOT about randomly breaking things. It is a structured experimental discipline with five steps.

Step 1 — Define 'steady state': What does 'normal' look like? Measure steady-state behavior: latency P50/P99, request throughput, error rate, error budget burn rate. Define SLOs for each metric. Example: 'Our payment service processes 2000 req/s with P99 latency < 100ms and error rate < 0.1%.'

Step 2 — Form a hypothesis: Predict what will happen when you introduce a fault. Example: 'If we kill one of three payment service instances, the load balancer will redirect traffic to the remaining two instances. P99 latency will increase by < 20ms, error rate will remain under 0.1%.' The hypothesis must be falsifiable.

Step 3 — Introduce controlled fault: Run the experiment on a production-like environment or production (if blast radius is controlled). Fault types: instance termination, network partition, latency injection, resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, disk), certificate expiration, DNS failure, database failover.

Step 4 — Measure vs. hypothesis: Compare the measured behavior against the steady-state baseline. Did P99 latency increase by more than 20ms? Did the error rate spike? Did the system recover within the expected window? Record all deviations.

Step 5 — Fix or escalate: If the hypothesis was wrong (the system behaved worse than expected), create a P1 ticket. The experiment has revealed a resilience gap. Fix the gap and re-run the experiment to confirm the fix. If the hypothesis was correct, document the result and move to the next experiment.

SAP Bridge: SAP systems rarely undergo chaos experiments. The closest analog is 'downtime planning' — calculating how long each component can be down without impacting operations. A chaos engineering approach would reveal: What happens to transaction processing when the enqueue server fails? Does message processing continue when the integration engine is disconnected? These are questions that chaos experiments answer.

Fix/Report</text><path d="M645,105 L645,130 L85,130 L85,105" class="ce-ed" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/><text class="ce-lbl" x="350" y="145">Iterate with increased blast radius</text></svg>

GameDays (Amazon's term) are scheduled events where teams simulate failure scenarios to test their systems and their response processes. Unlike automated chaos engineering, GameDays involve human participants: the on-call engineer, the incident commander, and system owners. GameDays test the intersection of software resilience and operational response.

GameDay planning checklist: (1) Define scenario — 'Primary database region becomes unavailable. Can we fail over within RTO? Do we lose data (RPO)?' (2) Define participants — incident commander, scribe, comms lead, subject matter experts. (3) Define duration — typically 2-4 hours with a hard stop. (4) Define success criteria — system met RTO/RPO during failover? Incident commander declared within 5 minutes? Comms sent to stakeholders within 15 minutes? (5) Simulate — use tools like AWS Fault Injection Simulator, GCP Chaos Mesh, or custom injection scripts. (6) Document — record timestamps of every decision, every action, and every system event. (7) Retro — what went well, what went wrong, what needs to be fixed before next GameDay.

Progressive complexity GameDay schedule: Month 1: kill single instance, replace health. Month 2: kill AZ, verify cross-AZ failover. Month 3: kill region, verify cross-region failover. Month 4: network partition between services, verify circuit breakers open. Month 5: database failover with active writes, verify RPO. Month 6: simulated DDoS/load spike, verify auto-scaling. Each GameDay increases scope, blast radius, and participant count.

GameDay anti-patterns: (1) Everyone in the same room (no real-world communication gaps). Solution: distribute participants across locations. (2) Over-communication during the incident. Solution: enforce a single incident commander; everyone else waits to be asked. (3) Fixing bugs during the GameDay. Solution: document the bug and fix it after; GameDays are for discovery, not remediation. (4) Simulating failures that can't happen in production. Solution: always use scenarios that are realistic for your architecture.

sections.title: Chaos Engineering at Netflix, Amazon, Google

description.value:
Each FAANG company practices chaos engineering differently, reflecting their architecture and culture. Understanding these differences shows breadth in a Staff+ interview.

Netflix (Chaos Monkey → Simian Army): Chaos Monkey randomly terminates EC2 instances in production during business hours. The principle: 'If you can't survive a random instance death, your system isn't resilient.' The Simian Army expanded to: Chaos Gorilla (kill an entire AZ), Chaos Kong (kill an entire region), Latency Monkey (inject network delays), Conformity Monkey (find instances that don't conform to best practices), Security Monkey (find security vulnerabilities), Janitor Monkey (clean up unused resources). Netflix runs chaos experiments 24/7 in production. Every service at Netflix must survive Chaos Monkey — it's a deployment requirement.

Amazon (GameDays + AWS Fault Injection Simulator): Amazon's approach is more structured and planned. Each team runs a GameDay at least once per quarter. Scenarios are defined in advance and documented as 'run-books.' AWS FIS is the managed service for injecting faults. Amazon's distinctive contribution: GameDays test not just the software but also the people and processes. An Amazon GameDay might include: 'The primary SRE is on PTO, the secondary SRE is in a meeting — who takes the page?' or 'The run-book hasn't been updated in 6 months — does it still work?'

Google (DiRT — Disaster Recovery Testing): Google's DiRT program simulates large-scale disasters: seismic events that take out a data center, supplier failures that cut off hardware supply, and even 'zombie apocalypse' scenarios for extreme testing. Google's approach emphasizes simultaneous multi-component failures (e.g., data center outage + DNS failure + SSL certificate expiry). The DiRT team develops scenarios that Google's SRE teams run through semi-annually. Google's unique insight: the most dangerous failures are correlated failures (multiple things failing at once), not single-point failures.

Blast radius is the most important concept in production chaos engineering. It defines the maximum impact of a failed experiment. The Staff+ engineer designs experiments with blast radius constraints that ensure the system can survive the experiment — even if the hypothesis is wrong.

Blast radius control techniques: (1) Instance-level: terminate a single instance of a service (replica count >= 3). The load balancer redirects traffic to remaining instances. Blast radius = 1/N of traffic. (2) AZ-level: terminate all instances in one availability zone. Requires cross-AZ deployment. Blast radius = traffic from one AZ (33% for 3-AZ deployment). (3) Request-level: inject latency or errors on a percentage of requests (1%, 5%, 10%). Requires request-scoped fault injection in the service mesh. (4) User-level: inject faults for a specific user cohort (internal users first, then 1%, 5%, etc.). Requires user-scoped feature flags or traffic shadowing.

Defense in depth for chaos experiments: (1) Experiment must be stoppable (kill switch). (2) Alert on experiment side-effects before the experiment's target metrics. (3) Automatically stop the experiment if error budget consumption exceeds N% in M minutes. (4) Run experiments in staging first, then production with the same fault configuration. (5) Never experiment on holidays, release days, or during known incident response.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Chaos engineering is a Staff+-level topic. Junior engineers talk about testing. Senior engineers talk about monitoring. Staff+ engineers talk about how to break your system intentionally to find unknown weaknesses. When asked 'How do you build resilient systems?' the Staff+ answer includes: 'We run chaos experiments on a schedule. Last quarter, we discovered that our circuit breaker timeout was too short for cross-region failover — a GameDay revealed it, and we fixed it.' This demonstrates operational maturity.
